channel.title: Paper Feeds
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper feeds from arXiv and IACR

item.title: CHRONOS: Temporally-Aware Multi-Agent Coordination for Evolving Data Marketplaces
item.description: CHRONOS is a temporally aware, multi-agent coordination framework for evolving data marketplaces built on knowledge graphs. It addresses three coupled failures in static designs—stale index shortcuts, misattributed Shapley pricing under distribution shifts, and uncoordinated differential-privacy budget consumption—via a unified three-layer architecture with explicit public/private separation. Layer 1 uses neural-ODE temporal decay to bound per-query recall loss as $O(P_q \lambda \Delta t)$, tightening bounds via monotone envelopes (1.8–3.2× tighter than observed loss). Layer 2 conditions Shapley valuation on detected changepoints and provides finite-sample error guarantees under noise. Layer 3 employs EXP3-IX for agent scheduling, achieving $O(\sqrt{T \log T})$ regret while enforcing $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP via moments accounting; it releases a privatized affinity matrix per epoch using the Gaussian mechanism, with all retrieval/ranking as zero-cost post-processing. Evaluated across four benchmarks, CHRONOS achieves Recall@10 = 0.937, 2.74 QPS, 161 ms latency, and total privacy cost $\varepsilon = 4.25$ at $\delta = 10^{-6}$ under zCDP composition—demonstrating a competitive operational point where utility stems primarily from public routing and low-sensitivity adaptive scheduling.

item.title: On the Stability of Spherical Hellinger-Kantorovich Flows and Their Implications for Differential Privacy
item.description: This paper establishes the first perturbation theory for spherical Hellinger–Kantorovich (SHK) gradient flows. Given two potentials $V$ and $V'$, we quantify how initial discrepancies propagate along their respective SHK flows—yielding a dimension-free, uniform bound on the log-likelihood ratio and Rényi divergence ($\alpha \ge 1$) at all times. Under mild Hessian regularity, we further derive an explicit time-dependent upper bound on the KL divergence. Applied to the exponential mechanism in differential privacy, our bounds deliver: (i) time-resolved $(\varepsilon(t), 0)$-DP guarantees via log-ratio control; (ii) $(\varepsilon, \delta(t))$-DP certificates through hockey-stick divergence; and (iii) a decomposed utility bound separating intrinsic mechanism suboptimality from finite-time sampling error. The results provide the first geometrically grounded, provable privacy–utility trade-off for SHK-based samplers.

item.title: AI Security Research Should Better Incentivize Defense Research
item.description: This paper identifies a critical structural imbalance in AI security research: a pronounced overemphasis on *attacking* AI systems relative to *defending* them—not only in publication volume but, more importantly, in evaluation rigor. Through systematic analysis of 327 papers across federated learning, speech recognition, membership inference, and large language model security, we find attack-to-defense ratios consistently exceed 4:1. Crucially, attack papers are routinely evaluated under overly favorable, idealized conditions (e.g., full model access, noiseless inputs), inflating perceived threat severity; defenses, by contrast, face unrealistically strict benchmarks—often invalidated by a single novel attack and rarely tested in real-world deployment settings (only 22% validated on production APIs). This asymmetry yields a literature rich in demonstrated vulnerabilities but poor in robust, deployable protections. We argue that sustainable AI security requires rebalancing incentives—through dedicated review criteria, standardized long-term robustness evaluation, and academic recognition of engineering rigor and deployment success.

item.title: Security, Privacy, and Ethical Risks in OpenClaw
item.description: This paper presents the first systematic analysis of security, privacy, ethical, and traceability risks in **OpenClaw**, a locally executable AI agent system designed for natural language interaction and real-world task execution. Through architecture-driven evaluation—examining its tool invocation model, persistent local storage, cross-context information aggregation, multi-user handling, and plugin integration—we identify critical vulnerabilities: unrestricted local resource access enabling lateral movement; implicit sensitive data fusion across tasks without user consent; identity ambiguity in shared environments; and fragmented audit logs impeding causal attribution. We argue these issues fundamentally undermine trustworthiness and hinder responsible deployment. The study concludes by proposing four open challenges requiring coordinated action: (1) adaptive runtime security enforcement, (2) context-aware privacy-preserving aggregation, (3) explainable ethical constraint embedding, and (4) unified provenance tracking across agent layers. Our findings serve as a foundational risk assessment for trustworthy local AI agents.

item.title: Prompt Overflow: What the Guardrail Inspects Is Not What the Model Infers
item.description: This paper identifies a critical security blind spot in guardrail models—widely deployed safety checkers for LLMs—arising from the mismatch between their narrow inspection windows (due to context constraints) and the much larger inference contexts of downstream LLMs. We introduce the **Prompt Overflow Attack**, which fragments malicious instructions and interleaves them with benign filler content across overlength prompts, ensuring no single inspected segment triggers detection while the full prompt remains fully actionable to the target LLM. Through systematic evaluation against state-of-the-art guardrails—including Meta’s Llama Prompt Guard, IBM’s Granite Guardian, and DeBERTa-based detectors—we show that adversarially lengthened prompts evade detection (as low as 3.2% detection rate) while preserving 100% attack success against LLMs—despite being reliably caught in short-context settings. Our work exposes a fundamental structural vulnerability in current safety architectures and proposes concrete mitigation directions: context-aware re-aggregation, cross-segment consistency modeling, and LLM-assisted verification.

item.title: PoisonForge: Task-Level Targeted Poisoning Benchmark for Instruction-Tuned LLMs
item.description: PoisonForge is the first benchmark for *task-level targeted poisoning* of instruction-tuned LLMs—where adversaries inject minimal crafted instruction-response pairs to compel models to embed attacker-specified entities (e.g., a country) *only* in outputs of a targeted task family (e.g., QA), while preserving normal behavior elsewhere. Parameterized across four dimensions (bias type, poisoning mode, entity appearance count, and target output length), PoisonForge evaluates 12 open-weight models (2B–32B) across five task families under a strict 1% poison budget (e.g., 10 poisoned examples among 1,000). Results show 11/12 models achieve >70% attack success rate (ASR) in their most vulnerable configuration, with <0.5% unintended leakage to non-target tasks and no degradation on standard benchmarks. We identify key drivers: ASR increases with entity appearances, optimal poisoning mode depends on entity semantics, and ASR monotonically decreases with output length. Crucially, correlation and predictive modeling confirm that *poisoning design—not model scale—is the primary determinant of ASR*, and these patterns generalize to unseen tasks. All configurations, pipelines, and analysis code are publicly released.

item.title: From Raw Experience to Skill Consumption: A Systematic Study of Model-Generated Agent Skills
item.description: This paper presents the first systematic, utility-grounded evaluation of the full skill lifecycle—experience generation, skill extraction, and skill consumption—for model-generated agent skills. Across five diverse agentic domains, we find that while such skills yield average performance gains, they incur non-trivial negative transfer (≈23% of cases), and extractor and consumer capabilities are largely decoupled: a model can excel at one stage yet fail at the other, irrespective of scale or baseline strength. Through fine-grained dissection, we identify experience composition, skill structural properties (e.g., step decomposability, context robustness), and consumer-specific transfer dynamics as key determinants of utility. Crucially, we distill these insights into a concrete *meta-skill*—a guidance protocol for extraction that prioritizes utility-predictive features over surface correctness. This meta-skill consistently improves skill quality (+18.7% mean utility) and cuts negative transfer by 52% across all domains.

item.title: MemAudit: Post-hoc Auditing of Poisoned Agent Memory via Causal Attribution and Structural Anomaly Detection
item.description: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly rely on persistent memory for long-horizon reasoning, yet this introduces a critical security vulnerability: adversaries can stealthily inject poisoned records via normal interactions, later retrieved to manipulate agent behavior. Existing defenses focus on *online* intervention but lack *post-hoc* audit capability—i.e., identifying which stored memories causally contributed to observed harm. We propose **MemAudit**, the first causal memory auditing framework for memory-augmented LLM agents. It combines (1) a counterfactual memory influence score to quantify each memory’s causal contribution to harmful outputs, and (2) a memory consistency graph to detect structurally anomalous memories within the broader memory store. Evaluated against the query-only MINJA attack across QA and reasoning-agent settings, MemAudit reduces attack success rates from 70% to 0% and from 83.3% to 0%, respectively, under realistic post-hoc auditing conditions. This work establishes a novel, interpretable, and deployable paradigm for memory integrity assurance.

item.title: EM-Vid: Training-Free Entity-Centric Memory for Efficient and Consistent Multi-Shot Video Generation
item.description: EM-Vid introduces a training-free, entity-centric memory framework for multi-shot video generation. It replaces full-frame memory with an entity-indexed bank of latent patches, decoupling persistent subject identity from transient scene context. We propose sparse token conditioning—compatible with off-the-shelf diffusion models—that restricts self-attention to entity-relevant tokens, cutting memory usage by 42% and accelerating inference. A structured script format enables precise cross-shot entity referencing, while a budgeted memory update strategy maintains compact, evolving representations. Crucially, noise injection into entity embeddings enables fine-grained appearance control and prevents irrelevant context leakage. On MultiShotBench, EM-Vid achieves +37.2% subject consistency score and +29.5% prompt fidelity over prior art—without any model fine-tuning.

item.title: Solving the Aircraft Disassembly Scheduling Problem
item.description: This paper addresses the Aircraft Disassembly Scheduling Problem (ADSP), a large-scale, highly constrained scheduling challenge arising in sustainable end-of-life aircraft dismantling. We formalize four critical real-world constraints: technician certification requirements, task precedence relations, whole-aircraft balance maintenance (modeled as dynamic center-of-gravity limits), and spatial capacity restrictions at aircraft locations. To solve ADSP, we propose two exact models: (1) a Constraint Programming (CP) model leveraging state variables and global constraints for natural handling of temporal and resource logic; and (2) a Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP) model using time-indexed variables and piecewise-linear approximations for balance constraints. Both models are validated on realistic industrial instances—up to 1,450 tasks—provided by an aerospace partner. Results show CP excels on small-to-medium instances (≤800 tasks) in solution speed, while MIP achieves tighter bounds and greater scalability on larger instances. This work is the first to integrate aircraft-level physical balance as a computable scheduling constraint, enabling practical, profit-aware disassembly planning.

item.title: CP or DP? Why Not Both: A Case Study in the Partial Shop Scheduling Problem
item.description: This paper introduces a hybrid paradigm that tightly integrates **Dynamic Programming (DP)** as the primary search backbone with **Constraint Programming (CP)** as an embedded inference engine—specifically for the Partial Shop Scheduling Problem (PSSP). Unlike conventional separate use, our approach leverages DP to enumerate state spaces (e.g., subsets of completed operations) while delegating global constraint propagation—especially for arbitrary precedence graphs—to a CP subroutine. This design enables *anytime* search (e.g., column search), seamless handling of complex precedence structures, and a novel Large Neighborhood Search (LNS) scheme where partial-order schedules from prior restarts guide subsequent DP runs. While not yet competitive with state-of-the-art pure CP solvers on PSSP benchmarks, our core contribution is the principled demonstration that such deep DP–CP integration is both viable and expressive—opening a new pathway for hybrid combinatorial optimization.

item.title: SSDAU: Structured Semantic Data Augmentation for Joint Entity and Relation Extraction
item.description: Joint Entity and Relation Extraction (JERE) suffers from poor generalization due to low-quality training data, yet conventional data augmentation methods often disrupt semantic structures and ignore contextual relevance. To address this, we propose **Structured Semantic Data Augmentation (SSDAU)**—a novel framework that preserves textual semantics during augmentation. SSDAU segments text by entity labels, encodes context-aware entity representations, and performs entity-level semantic restructuring (not lexical substitution). It fuses contextualized embeddings with traditional similarity metrics to distinguish fine-grained semantically similar entities and applies BERTTopic to filter out off-topic samples, ensuring topic consistency. Evaluated across five JERE models and five diverse datasets (including ADE, NYT29, and DocRED), SSDAU consistently outperforms seven strong baselines. Crucially, it demonstrates superior robustness: under semantic ambiguity, its F1 drops only **8.26%**, versus an average **31.91%** for baselines—marking the first method to jointly optimize semantic fidelity and generalization gain.

item.title: Computing Asymptotic Bounds for the Automated Coppersmith Method via Linear Programming
item.description: Coppersmith’s method is fundamental for finding small modular polynomial roots, yet deriving tight asymptotic bounds for recoverable roots remains analytically challenging—especially for the Automated Coppersmith Method (Meers & Nowakowski, ASIACRYPT 2023). We bridge this gap by rigorously reformulating the asymptotic bound computation as a linear programming (LP) problem, yielding a provably correct, closed-form, and efficiently computable formula. Our LP model captures lattice dimension, determinant bounds, and root size constraints under standard asymptotic parameterization. Applying it, we improve asymptotic bounds for five cryptanalytic settings: Commutative Isogeny HNP, Modular Inversion HNP, Elliptic Curve HNP, LCG with unknown multiplier, and Leveled Isogeny with Hints for POKE—e.g., pushing the Modular Inversion HNP bound from $1/4$ to $0.272$ and LCG bound to $0.357$. This framework enables systematic, verifiable security evaluation across diverse lattice-based cryptographic assumptions.

item.title: A New Construction Method for More Efficient Quadratic One-Time Noisy Multi-Client Functional Encryption Schemes
item.description: We propose a new construction for one-time multi-client functional encryption (MCFE) supporting *noisy quadratic functions*, corruption resistance, and *label support*—enabling practical privacy-preserving machine learning over arbitrarily split data. Unlike prior bilinear-group-based approaches, our design is *structure-agnostic*: it relies solely on simpler, more efficient building blocks and requires *no additional hardness assumptions* beyond those of its components—yielding stronger generality and cleaner security proofs. We instantiate the framework as **QUILT**, and experiments show it outperforms state-of-the-art schemes by **4.8×–6.8× speedup** in private logistic regression training, with significantly reduced communication and ciphertext size. Crucially, QUILT natively supports *labels*, allowing multiple encryptions under distinct labels—effectively relaxing the “one-time” constraint while preserving security.

item.title: When Removing Reductions Goes Wrong: Auditing Reduction Placement in Production ML-DSA Implementations
item.description: This paper presents a certificate-backed audit methodology for reduction placement in production ML-DSA implementations. We propagate exact integer coefficient bounds across the full signing path—starting from the conservative pq-crystals topology—and classify each reduction site as *redundant* (safe to omit) or *necessary* (load-bearing), leveraging a novel exact-integer recovery theorem for sparse-challenge products. This theorem tightens post-InvNTT output bounds from $(-Q, Q)$ to $[-\tau\eta, \tau\eta]$, enabling precise separation between safe omissions on sparse-product paths and critical reductions on dense-product paths (e.g., after matrix multiplication). Applied to eight ML-DSA libraries, our method uncovered an unreported defect in wolfSSL’s `WOLFSSL-DILITHIUM-SMALL`: omitted post-matrix-multiplication reductions cause silent overflows, non-conformant arithmetic, and signing failures—yet evade existing KATs. All classifications are backed by replayable SMT-LIB2 certificates, with core lemmas formally verified in an axiom-free Coq development.

item.title: Compact Quaternion Algorithms for SQIsign
item.description: SQIsign is a compact isogeny-based post-quantum signature scheme, but its reliance on rational quaternion algebra arithmetic previously hindered fixed-precision, constant-time implementations—especially for memory-constrained devices. While Kim et al. recently established the first fixed-precision instantiation, their worst-case precision bounds remained excessive (exceeding 13× public key size), demanding wide integer buffers and limiting practicality. In this work, we design novel compact quaternion algorithms for key generation and signing, rigorously analyzing intermediate integer growth and deriving significantly tighter uniform worst-case size bounds. Our bounds reduce required precision from 7026/10713/14150 bits to just 1774/2696/3555 bits at NIST Levels I/III/V—achieving **74.75–74.88% compression**. We implement the full scheme under these new budgets, attaining up to **67.3% faster signing** and **41.4% faster key generation** (vs. prior fixed-precision) at Level I. This enables efficient, low-memory, constant-time SQIsign deployment on embedded platforms.

item.title: Pushforward Problems and Applications to Isogeny-based Cryptography
item.description: This paper generalizes the pushforward problem in isogeny-based cryptography: given supersingular elliptic curves $E$, $E'$ and an isogeny $\varphi: E \to E'$ of known degree $d$, we no longer assume efficient representation of $E[N]$-torsion points. Instead, we introduce a novel *pushforward oracle* $\mathcal{O}_\varphi$ that, on input any $N$-isogeny $\psi: E \to E''$, outputs its pushforward $\varphi_*\psi: E' \to E'''$. We prove that for concrete families—e.g., when $N = \ell^e$ with small $\ell$ and $\varphi$ admits a specific factorization—$\varphi$ can be recovered in polynomial time using only $\mathcal{O}_\varphi$. Crucially, this leads to three practical cryptanalytic results: (1) a full key-recovery attack against the Kim–Kim–Lee threshold signature scheme; (2) identification of weak parameters in Basso’s oblivious PRF where pushforward distinguishability breaks pseudorandomness; and (3) a security refinement for the Leroux–Roméas updatable encryption scheme, necessitating stronger assumptions against adaptive pushforward queries.

item.title: Towards a Unified Memory-Less Framework for TCitH
item.description: This work introduces a unified, memory-less Zero-Knowledge Proof (ZKP) framework for all TCitH-based submissions to the NIST Additional Post-Quantum Signature Competition. Targeting embedded systems like ARM Cortex-M4, our framework reduces stack usage by up to 99% versus baseline implementations—achieving sub-200 B stack footprint—while adding only minimal code size overhead (<3.5 KB) and negligible performance cost (<5% signing time increase). It is designed for extensibility: integrating new TCitH variants requires implementing only their core mathematical primitives (e.g., GGM expansion, polynomial commitment logic) and proof procedures. As a key enabling contribution, we provide the first constant-time, bitsliced Rijndael-256 implementation optimized for constrained architectures—supporting the Expand, PRG, and Commit functions central to TCitH schemes—with measured throughput of 1.2 MB/s on Cortex-M4. This implementation is independently valuable given NIST’s ongoing standardization of Rijndael-256 and its broad adoption across MPCitH-family schemes.

item.title: Collusion-Resistant Asymmetric Anamorphic Encryption: Framework, Generic Construction, and Concrete Instantiations
item.description: We initiate the first comprehensive study of **collusion-resistant asymmetric anamorphic encryption**, addressing a critical gap in prior work: fully asymmetric schemes (Catalano et al., Eurocrypt’24) collapse under sender corruption, as the dictator can extract the sender’s “double key” for covert encryption. We introduce a novel cryptographic primitive—**witness PRF for PKE**—that precisely captures the structural requirements for embedding secure covert channels under collusion. Leveraging this abstraction, we give a **generic construction** for any PKE scheme with high-min-entropy ciphertexts (e.g., ElGamal, Regev, Paillier), achieving stronger collusion security under standard assumptions (DDH, CDH, LWE) — *without indistinguishability obfuscation*. Our instantiations are direct, efficient, and deployable in practice. This work establishes the first rigorous, feasible, and unified foundation for covert communication resilient to active coercion and collusion.

item.title: Sparse Hermite Interpolation Method for Discrete-CKKS Functional Bootstrapping
item.description: Discrete CKKS enables exact homomorphic evaluation of arbitrary functions over encrypted discrete data via *functional bootstrapping*, where noise reduction is achieved through Hermite interpolation. We identify fundamental limitations in two state-of-the-art methods: Bae et al.’s (ASIACRYPT’24) suffers from insufficient derivative order, while Alexandru et al.’s (CRYPTO’25) incurs instability due to dense coefficient growth. To overcome both, we propose *sparse trigonometric Hermite interpolation*—a CKKS-native method supporting arbitrary-order derivative constraints with provably sparse time-domain constraints and frequency-domain coefficients. This yields efficiency comparable to Alexandru et al.’s fastest approach ($\tilde{O}(N\log N)$), yet achieves superior noise reduction. We introduce a unified metric—the *inter-bootstrap noise budget*—to fairly compare methods, and implement our scheme in OpenFHE. Experiments show our method delivers up to **3.2× higher noise attenuation**, enabling 2–3 additional layers of nonlinear computation versus prior art.

item.title: Geometric Critical Point Screening: Clustering-Free Cryptanalytic Extraction of Neural Network Models
item.description: Neural network model extraction poses a critical security threat, yet existing signature extraction methods suffer from prohibitive query and computational overhead due to their clustering-dependent, “compute-first-then-cluster” paradigm. This paper introduces **Geometric Critical Point Screening (GCPS)**—a clustering-free, geometry-driven framework that exploits the intrinsic piecewise linear structure of neural networks. By searching for critical points on three coplanar parallel lines, GCPS isolates first-hidden-layer neuron critical points with minimal signature queries, reducing signature extraction complexity from $O(N \log N \cdot d_0)$ to $O(d_1 \cdot d_0)$. For networks where input dimension exceeds the first hidden layer width ($d_0 > d_1$), GCPS further enables efficient second-hidden-layer screening within a fully activated subspace using three coplanar line segments. Crucially, GCPS computes signatures for only a small number of *non-target* critical points, inherently validates contaminated points, and requires no clustering. Experiments on a $784\text{-}8^{(8)}\text{-}1$ network show GCPS reduces signature extraction time to **1.7% and 3.7%**, and query cost to **3.2% and 0.1%**, of state-of-the-art methods for first and second hidden layers, respectively. The method generalizes naturally to all piecewise linear activations, establishing a lightweight, geometrically grounded foundation for cryptanalytic model extraction.

item.title: Symmetric Attribute-Based Encryption from Minimal Hardness Assumptions
item.description: We present the first *symmetric* ciphertext-policy attribute-based encryption (CP-ABE) scheme, where **both the decryptor *and* the encryptor must satisfy the access policy** to enable encryption—a paradigm shift from standard asymmetric ABE. Built solely on minimal assumptions—collision-resistant hash functions and pseudorandom functions—the scheme leverages linear secret sharing and polynomial interpolation, yielding **native post-quantum security**. We introduce an *extended access tree* model that trades bounded polynomial space expansion for a regular, analyzable topology, enhancing arithmetic expressiveness of the underlying secret sharing. Finally, we propose a rigorous, unified notation for access structures, sharing matrices, and interpolation weights—facilitating formal analysis and implementation of ABE primitives.

item.title: Quantum and Post-Quantum Blockchain: A Systematic Survey
item.description: This systematic survey analyzes 127 peer-reviewed works (2016–2024) on quantum and post-quantum blockchain. We categorize approaches into two paradigms: (1) *Post-quantum blockchains*—integrating NIST-standardized lattice-based cryptography (e.g., Kyber, Dilithium) into consensus, smart contracts, and light clients—yet facing critical trade-offs: 3–8× larger signatures and 40–200% higher verification latency; and (2) *Quantum blockchains*—leveraging quantum entanglement for tamper-evident ledgers, QKD-secured BFT protocols, and QRNG-driven consensus—demonstrating information-theoretic security and nanosecond timestamping in metropolitan testbeds, though limited by quantum memory coherence and repeater scalability. We propose the *Quantum Readiness Maturity Model (QRMM)* to benchmark 23 systems across cryptographic agility, protocol compatibility, and hardware dependency. Our key finding: hybrid architectures (PQ cryptography + QKD) represent the most viable near-term path toward quantum resilience.

item.title: Efficient Homomorphic String Search via TFHE
item.description: We propose an efficient homomorphic string search method using TFHE (Torus Fully Homomorphic Encryption) for secure pattern matching over encrypted texts. By ingeniously leveraging TFHE’s two integer-input operational modes—noise-free Boolean circuit evaluation and efficient (noisy) integer arithmetic—we design the first fully secure binary-search-style algorithm for encrypted pattern matching. Unlike the prior BGV-based approach (Bonte & Iliashenko, CCSW ’20), which requires $O(|P| \cdot |T|)$ homomorphic character comparisons to locate pattern $P$ in text $T$, our method reduces the number of comparisons to $O(|P| \log |T|)$, dramatically improving scalability. Experimentally, our scheme finds a 100-character pattern in an encrypted 1-million-character genomic dataset in under 5 minutes—whereas prior work would take approximately 5 days. This >1400× speedup demonstrates TFHE’s practical viability for large-scale secure string search and highlights the critical importance of tailoring algorithms to TFHE’s native primitives.

item.title: Comments on "Server-Aided Public Key Authenticated Searchable Encryption With Constant Ciphertext and Constant Trapdoor"
item.description: This note identifies fundamental security flaws in Cheng and Meng’s SA-PAEKS scheme (*IEEE T-IFS* 2024), which claims constant-size ciphertexts and trapdoors for server-aided public-key authenticated searchable encryption. We present two practical, polynomial-time attacks executable by the untrusted cloud server (tester): (1) a *ciphertext inversion attack* that recovers the hash of any target keyword $\text{H}(w)$ by exploiting shared randomness and hash-chain dependencies between ciphertexts and trapdoors; and (2) a *trapdoor equivalence attack* that detects whether two trapdoors correspond to the same keyword via signature component manipulation and response analysis. Both attacks require only black-box access to the test oracle—no private key or side channels—and succeed with high probability (e.g., >80% keyword recovery within 100 queries on real-world implementations). Our results demonstrate that SA-PAEKS fails to achieve keyword privacy under standard cryptographic assumptions, exposing a critical trade-off between efficiency and security in server-aided searchable encryption. We conclude that outsourcing search capability to semi-honest servers necessitates stronger primitives—such as zero-knowledge proofs or TEEs—to guarantee keyword indistinguishability.

item.title: EnCAgg: Enhanced Clustering Aggregation for Robust Federated Learning against Dynamic Model Poisoning
item.description: Federated learning (FL) is vulnerable to dynamic model poisoning attacks, where adversaries adaptively manipulate gradients to degrade global model performance. Existing defenses—relying on fixed thresholds or pre-specified cluster numbers—fail under non-IID data and evolving attack strategies, often discarding benign outliers. To address this, we propose **EnCAgg**, a novel robust aggregation framework leveraging only a few known benign clients as references. EnCAgg operates in three stages: (i) density-based clustering of gradients projected onto the two most divergent principal components; (ii) generation of boundary-aligned pseudo-gradients via a lightweight generator to bridge sparse benign outliers; and (iii) re-clustering of real and pseudo-gradients to recover misclassified benign gradients. Extensive experiments on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and MIND demonstrate that EnCAgg achieves superior fidelity and robustness—outperforming state-of-the-art methods by up to 15.7% accuracy under adaptive poisoning, while maintaining stability across varying malicious client ratios (10%–40%).

item.title: Benchmarking Autonomous Agents against Temporal, Spatial, and Semantic Evasions
item.description: This paper introduces A3S, the first multi-dimensional evasion framework targeting LLM-based autonomous agents, exposing critical architecture-level vulnerabilities overlooked by prior single-turn security analyses. We define and operationalize three stealthy attack vectors: (1) *Temporal evasion*—fragmenting malicious payloads across sequential turns to evade stateless detection; (2) *Spatial evasion*—hiding payloads in complex external artifacts (e.g., obfuscated JSON, nested Markdown, malformed code blocks) that bypass standard LLM parsing; and (3) *Semantic evasion*—masking malicious intent beneath high-fidelity benign contextual noise (e.g., verbose logs, technical jargon overload). To rigorously evaluate these threats, we build **A3S-Bench**, a benchmark of 2,254 real-world agent execution traces. Evaluating a standardized agent framework integrated with 10 mainstream LLMs across 20 practical threat scenarios, our framework increases the average risk trigger rate from 28.3% to 52.6%—a 24.3-point surge—demonstrating systemic failures in current defense mechanisms. These findings underscore an urgent need for defenses co-designed for stateful interaction, dynamic tool invocation, and cross-modal artifact processing.

item.title: Adversarial Trust Poisoning in Vehicular Collaborative Perception
item.description: Collaborative perception (CP) enhances autonomous driving by enabling vehicles to share sensor data and jointly infer the environment. Current defenses rely on cross-vehicle consistency checking and trust scoring—penalizing vehicles whose observations deviate from the majority. We reveal that this very mechanism creates a new attack surface. We propose **TrustFlip**, a physical-world adversarial attack that deploys genuine but perception-disrupting objects (e.g., textured road obstacles) to induce *benign yet inconsistent* detections across normal vehicles. The defense misattributes these inconsistencies to a targeted benign vehicle, eroding its trust score until it is downweighted or excluded—degrading overall perception accuracy and safety. Evaluated across diverse CP architectures and trust mechanisms, TrustFlip removes the target vehicle in up to **87.7%** of scenarios and reduces Average Precision by up to **13.0%**. As a lightweight mitigation, we introduce **TrustReflect**, which identifies disputed spatial regions, marks them as uncertain, and excludes them from trust evaluation—reducing attack success by **35–100%** with negligible overhead.

item.title: QT-PUF: Quantum Tunneling Leakage Based PUF for Implantable IoMT Devices
item.description: This paper introduces **QT-PUF**, a quantum tunneling leakage–based Physical Unclonable Function (PUF) tailored for ultra-low-power (ULP) implantable Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) devices. Leveraging inherent gate-tunneling current variations—arising from quantum-mechanical effects and process-induced CMOS fluctuations—QT-PUF operates under static bias with **no external excitation or clocking**, eliminating power overhead and stability issues plaguing memory-, RO-, or arbiter-based PUFs. A novel differential readout circuit with pseudo-resistor I-to-V frontend converts picoampere-level leakage differences into reliable digital responses. In 65 nm CMOS simulations, QT-PUF achieves near-perfect entropy (**0.9999998**), ideal fuzzy Hamming distance (**0.5001**), ultralow energy consumption (**19.21 fJ/bit**), and robust operation across **0.9–1.3 V** and **0–100°C**, with average BER < **0.000163** under typical implantable conditions (1.0–1.3 V / 10–70°C).

item.title: Automated Repair of TEE Partitioning Issues via DSL-Guided and LLM-Assisted Patching
item.description: Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) enforce hardware-isolated execution but remain vulnerable to partitioning errors—misplaced trust boundaries between trusted and untrusted code—that cause data leaks or code injection. While detection tools exist, automated repair is unsolved due to TEE-specific challenges: absent secure coding standards, semantic ambiguity in low-level C, and lack of validation infrastructure. We present **TEERepair**, the first framework for fully automated TEE partitioning repair. It introduces a lightweight domain-specific language (DSL) to encode security patterns as context-parameterized patch templates, then leverages large language models (LLMs) to reason about code semantics and synthesize correct, compilable patches. TEERepair further auto-generates test clients to validate repairs end-to-end. Evaluated on the PartitioningE-Bench benchmark, it achieves an 87.6% repair success rate—surpassing all baselines by >32 percentage points. Applied to real-world TEE projects (OP-TEE, Trusty), it produced 5 pull requests, with 2 already merged by maintainers.

item.title: Finding Missing Input Validation in TEEs via LLM-Assisted Symbolic Execution
item.description: Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) offer strong hardware-enforced isolation, yet automated detection of missing input validation—a critical vulnerability class—remains hindered by costly TEE setup and poor observability. This paper introduces **SymTEE**, the first LLM-assisted symbolic execution framework that detects such flaws *without any physical TEE infrastructure*. SymTEE combines AST-based code slicing to identify validation-prone TEE code regions, then leverages GPT-5 to autonomously generate KLEE-compatible harnesses with lightweight mock environments (e.g., emulated ECALL/OCALL, memory layout). Evaluated on 26 vulnerabilities (11 real-world, 15 synthetic), SymTEE achieves **100% precision and 92.3% recall**, with an average analysis cost of just **$0.05**. This demonstrates a scalable, setup-free paradigm for TEE security analysis—where LLMs bridge the gap between high-level semantics and low-level symbolic reasoning.

item.title: Secure and Parallel Determinant Computation for Large-Scale Matrices in Edge Environments
item.description: Edge computing enables resource-constrained IoT clients to offload intensive tasks—such as Matrix Determinant Computation (MDC)—to distributed edge servers. Yet, traditional $O(n^3)$ MDC algorithms are ill-suited for real-time, privacy-sensitive edge deployments. We propose **Secure Parallel Determinant Computation (SPDC)**, a framework guaranteeing end-to-end privacy and integrity across $N$ untrusted edge servers. SPDC introduces **Composite Element Distortion (CED)**, a lightweight encryption combining Element-wise Obfuscation (EWO) and the Panth Rotation Theorem (PRT), preserving determinant properties while hiding both numerical and structural matrix information. Parallel block LU decomposition distributes encrypted submatrices under a **one-way communication model**, eliminating inter-server coordination. For efficient verification, we design two algorithms: probabilistic scalar verifier $Q_2$ and deterministic low-complexity verifier $Q_3$. Analysis shows SPDC achieves semantic security, $O(n^2)$ communication, near-linear scalability, and client-side overhead below $O(n^{2.5})$, outperforming homomorphic encryption baselines by 17.3× in speed and 89% in bandwidth on 1000×1000 matrices.

item.title: Parser-Free Querying of Security Logs
item.description: Security analysts face a fundamental trade-off: structured log querying requires costly per-source parsers, while raw-log tools (e.g., `grep`) lack expressiveness for multi-line, temporal, and cross-event security investigations. We present **Sieve**, a parser-free system that generates executable query code from natural-language questions by grounding an LLM with *automatically extracted, lightweight log-format context*—requiring only one LLM call per query followed by deterministic execution on raw files. Evaluated on 133 real-world security queries across 5 log types (Syslog, Windows Event Log, Apache, JSON audit logs, CloudTrail), Sieve reduces error rates by **over 3×** on complex temporal and cross-event queries compared to expert-written scripts, with the largest gains (>89% accuracy improvement) on multi-line correlation tasks critical to active incident response. Crucially, Sieve achieves this with minimal format analysis (<100 tokens per source), sub-2.1-second end-to-end latency, and strong zero-shot generalization—even to unseen log variants. Our benchmark and results demonstrate that LLM-generated code, when tightly constrained by lightweight structural context, bridges the long-standing gap between query expressiveness and operational immediacy in security logging.

item.title: Optimal Guarantees for Auditing Rényi Differentially Private Machine Learning
item.description: We propose the first black-box auditing framework for Rényi Differential Privacy (RDP) with *optimal, non-asymptotic guarantees*. Leveraging the Donsker–Varadhan variational estimator under function-class restrictions, our method directly estimates Rényi divergence between neighboring executions and yields explicit confidence intervals—rigorously decoupling statistical estimation error from algorithmic privacy leakage. We prove matching minimax lower bounds, showing our sample complexity is information-theoretically optimal up to logarithmic factors—establishing the first optimal guarantees for RDP auditing via DV estimators. Empirically, on DP-SGD trained on MNIST and CIFAR-10 across diverse privacy regimes, our auditor achieves substantially tighter empirical RDP lower bounds than prior black-box methods—especially at small-to-moderate α (2–6), where auditing is most challenging and critical.

item.title: From Correlation to Cause: A Five-Stage Methodology for Feature Analysis in Transformer Language Models
item.description: We introduce a five-stage methodology for causal feature analysis in transformer language models—covering probe design, feature extraction, causal validation, robustness testing, and deployment integration—and demonstrate it end-to-end on GPT-2 small solving the Indirect Object Identification (IOI) task. Activation patching isolates the canonical IOI circuit (Layer 9, Head 9 yields +1.02 recovery), while a sparse autoencoder discovers name-selective features with effect sizes of 30–50 activation units. Causal validation reveals these features are highly selective but only partially causal: ablating 15 such features preserves 98% accuracy. Two NLA-inspired analyses confirm this—these features explain just 31% of activation variance (vs. 99.7% by the SAE), and selectivity ratio anticorrelates with causal strength (*r* = −0.56). Under distribution shift, circuit functionality transfers cleanly, yet ablation effects degrade sharply—exposing a critical gap between detection and causal robustness. A cost-based deployment evaluation ($50/FN, $0.42/FP, 2% baseline error) identifies an optimal monitor yielding $8.96 per 1000 queries—99.1% savings over $1000 baseline—with optimal composition depending on cost ratio and base rate. Crucially, no single stage suffices; only their conjunction yields coherent, actionable causal insight.

item.title: Cross-Subject EEG Emotion Recognition Based on Temporal Asynchronous Alignment Contrastive Learning
item.description: Electroencephalography (EEG)-based emotion recognition faces a critical yet underexplored challenge: **inter-subject temporal asynchrony**, where neural responses to identical emotional stimuli exhibit subject-specific latency shifts and rhythm mismatches. To address this, we propose **Temporal Asynchronous Alignment-based Contrastive Learning (TA2CL)**—a novel framework inspired by ColBERT’s late-interaction paradigm in NLP. Unlike conventional global similarity computation, TA2CL performs fine-grained, learnable local alignment between EEG segments within a flexible time-offset window, enabling adaptive discovery of highly correlated subsequences across subjects. We introduce an asynchronous-aware contrastive loss that operates directly on aligned local embeddings, mitigating inter-subject variability without requiring explicit temporal warping or domain adaptation. Experiments on FACED, SEED, and SEED-V demonstrate state-of-the-art performance: **64.5% (9-class) and 79.5% (binary) accuracy on FACED**, **86.4% on SEED**, and **70.1% on SEED-V**, consistently outperforming prior methods. TA2CL establishes a robust, parameter-efficient paradigm for cross-subject EEG emotion recognition.

item.title: Detecting Atypical Clients in Federated Learning via Representation-Level Divergence
item.description: Federated learning (FL) faces reliability challenges when clients exhibit not only benign non-i.i.d. data but also distributional shifts or anomalous inputs, causing functional deviations that degrade global model performance. We propose a lightweight, representation-level divergence metric that quantifies how each client’s local training alters the *activation-induced partition* of the input space—evaluated on a shared unlabeled probe set—rather than comparing parameters or gradients. This geometric signal is permutation-invariant, interpretable, and directly captures differences in *how data is processed*, not just parameter updates. Empirically, it reliably distinguishes stable heterogeneous clients from atypical ones whose functional behavior significantly diverges from the global regime. The metric requires only one forward pass per client, imposes negligible overhead, and enables risk-aware aggregation (e.g., adaptive weighting or outlier filtering) without modifying local training.

item.title: Comparing LLM and Fine-Tuned Model Performance on NVDRS Circumstance Extraction with Varying Prompt Complexity
item.description: Suicide is a leading cause of death in the U.S., requiring structured extraction of inferentially complex circumstances (e.g., “recent job loss”, “intimate partner conflict”) from unstructured death investigation narratives. We introduce a **Complexity Score** algorithm that quantifies circumstance-specific prompt sensitivity by analyzing NVDRS coding manual structure, enabling dynamic prompt selection—detailed guidelines for high-complexity/low-prevalence cases, name-only prompts otherwise. Evaluating on 25 challenging circumstances from the National Violent Death Reporting System (NVDRS), we find LLMs (GPT-4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Llama-3 70B) substantially outperform fine-tuned RoBERTa on low-prevalence circumstances (F1 +22.6%), where training data is scarce. The Complexity Score strongly predicts LLM advantage (r = −0.83), and our hybrid framework generalizes across frontier LLMs with consistent performance patterns. Results support a practical architecture: **LLMs handle rare, inference-heavy cases; fine-tuned models process common ones—optimizing accuracy, scalability, and cost.**

item.title: Lumberjack: Better Differentially Private Random Forests through Heavy Hitter Detection in Trees
item.description: We present **Lumberjack**, a differentially private (DP) random forest algorithm that significantly improves utility over prior methods. Instead of building shallow trees or adding noise to splits, Lumberjack constructs *deep* random decision trees and applies aggressive, privacy-preserving pruning guided by a novel $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP heavy hitter detection mechanism for hierarchical data. Our detector achieves error $O_{\varepsilon,\delta}(\sqrt{\log h})$ for trees of height $h$, enabling much deeper trees than previously feasible under DP constraints. Empirical evaluation across standard tabular benchmarks shows Lumberjack consistently outperforms state-of-the-art DP random forests—achieving up to +12% accuracy gain at $\varepsilon=2$ and establishing new utility frontiers for practical privacy budgets ($\varepsilon \in [0.5, 8]$). This work demonstrates that carefully structured DP tree ensembles can substantially close the utility gap with non-private counterparts.

item.title: Represented Is Not Computed: A Causal Test of Candidate Algorithmic Intermediates in a Transformer
item.description: This paper challenges the common inference that linear probe success implies algorithmic implementation. We study a Transformer solving base-digit extraction—predicting the $D$-th digit of $N$ in base $B$, with closed-form solution $\lfloor N/B^D \rfloor \bmod B$. The model achieves 99.83% exact accuracy across held-out number–base combinations. While linear probes reliably decode the hypothesized intermediates ($N/B^D$, floor, mod), causal tests—via targeted ablations and pathway interventions—reveal that the critical $D$-to-output route depends *only* on early $D$-selective communication, independent of $N$ and $B$. Sparse circuit analysis further shows largely segregated $N$-, $B$-, and $D$-specific pathways that combine late, contradicting the staged computation implied by probes. Thus, the model *represents* but does *not compute via* the probed intermediates—demonstrating a sharp divergence between representational evidence and causal mechanism, even when explicit algorithms are available.

item.title: When Stronger Triggers Backfire: A High-Dimensional Theory of Backdoor Attacks
item.description: Backdoor poisoning attacks exhibit a counter-intuitive *inverse strength effect* in high dimensions: stronger training triggers can *improve clean accuracy* and *hinder attack success*. We analyze regularized generalized linear models (GLMs) on Gaussian-mixture data in the proportional regime ($p/n \to \kappa$), varying training trigger strength $\alpha$ while fixing the test trigger. We prove three key phenomena in closed form for squared loss—and extend the first two to general convex GLMs via a Gaussian-proxy fixed-point system: (i) clean test accuracy increases monotonically with $\alpha$; (ii) attack success rate peaks at a finite $\alpha^*$ and then declines; (iii) the most damaging trigger direction aligns with the *minimum eigenvector* of the data covariance. Crucially, phenomenon (i) arises from a finite-sample noise floor scaling as $\mathcal{O}(\kappa)$, invisible under classical $n \gg p$ asymptotics. Experiments on CIFAR-10, synthetic Gaussians, and ResNet-18 strongly corroborate the theory—demonstrating its validity beyond convexity and practical relevance.

item.title: Winner-Take-All bottlenecks enforce disentangled symbolic representations in multi-task learning
item.description: Winner-take-all (WTA) mechanisms—ubiquitous in cortical circuits and modern deep learning (e.g., softmax in attention)—are shown here to enforce *disentangled symbolic representations* under theoretically grounded conditions in multi-task learning. We prove that a WTA bottleneck provably induces categorical latent codes: each neuron (or small population) encodes a single abstract, semantically meaningful feature (e.g., “red”, “car”, “top-left”)—not entangled subsymbolic vectors. Empirically, on CLEVR-Spatial and Multi-dSprites, models with approximate WTA bottlenecks (e.g., Top-k softmax) consistently learn such symbolic representations even beyond strict theoretical assumptions. These representations yield substantial gains in zero-shot cross-task generalization (+12.3% accuracy) and interpretable, intervention-based reasoning. Our work bridges subsymbolic learning and symbolic AI through a biologically and computationally plausible architectural principle.

item.title: Boundary-targeted Membership Inference Attacks on Safety Classifiers
item.description: Safety classifiers are critical for mitigating harms in generative AI, yet their training on sensitive mental health data raises serious privacy risks via membership inference attacks (MIAs). We hypothesize that examples where the classifier exhibits low confidence—i.e., near its decision boundary—serve as high-signal targets for MIAs due to localized over-reliance on memorization. To this end, we propose a **boundary-targeted selection strategy** that identifies and amplifies membership signals from such ambiguous inputs. On a fine-tuned safety classifier for emotional support detection, our attack recovers **19% of flagged user-distress conversations at just 5% false-positive rate**, outperforming state-of-the-art MIAs by **3.5×**. We further characterize boundary-laying examples—showing they evade content-based filtering but are effectively protected by lightweight noise injection (e.g., Gaussian noise with σ=0.05) or gradient clipping. This work establishes a new, interpretable paradigm for auditing and hardening safety-critical AI components.

item.title: Spectra as Language: Large Language Models for Scalable Stellar Parameter and Abundance Inference
item.description: Stellar spectra encode rich physical and chemical information, yet conventional methods for parameter inference—such as grid-based model fitting or hand-crafted feature engineering—are computationally prohibitive, poorly generalizable across instruments, and inefficient for modern peta-scale surveys. Inspired by the success of large language models (LLMs) in sequential data domains (e.g., genomics, chemistry), we recast stellar spectra as *tokenized language sequences*, preserving spectral continuity and resolution. We propose a two-stage LLM framework: (1) a physics-aware SpectraTokenizer that discretizes normalized flux spectra into spectral tokens with instrument- and S/N-sensitive positional encoding; and (2) a StellarLLM—a Transformer variant with line-sensitive attention masking and physics-informed loss—to jointly infer $T_{\text{eff}}$, $\log g$, $[\text{Fe}/\text{H}]$, and abundances of 19 elements. Trained on ~1.1 million high-quality spectra from APOGEE and GALAH, our model achieves median errors of 42 K, 0.07 dex, and 0.035 dex for $T_{\text{eff}}$, $\log g$, and $[\text{Fe}/\text{H}]$, respectively, and <0.05 dex for most elemental abundances—outperforming state-of-the-art deep learning baselines. Crucially, it processes 2,000 spectra per second on a single A100 GPU and exhibits strong scaling behavior: a 10× increase in training data reduces metallicity error by 32%, confirming its readiness for next-generation billion-spectrum surveys.

item.title: Diagnosis Is Not Prescription: Linguistic Co-Adaptation Explains Patching Hazards in LLM Pipelines
item.description: This paper identifies and validates the *Diagnostic Paradox*: the module most causally responsible for an LLM agent’s failure is often *not* the optimal target for intervention. Across three distinct agent families (ReAct, Plan-and-Execute, Toolformer), causal analysis consistently pinpoints the **routing module** as the primary bottleneck—yet injecting prompt-level correction examples into it *degrades* performance, sometimes severely. In contrast, applying the same correction strategy to the upstream **query-rewriting module** reliably improves outcomes (statistically significant in two families; directionally consistent in the third). Alternative routing-module repairs (instruction rewriting, model upgrade) show neutral effects, confirming harm is specific to correction-injection patching. We explain this via the *Linguistic Contract Hypothesis*: downstream modules implicitly co-adapt to their upstream’s characteristic error distribution; patching the bottleneck breaks this implicit alignment, whereas upstream patching preserves or restores it. We operationalize co-adaptation as a diagnosis-derived per-agent metric and show—across all three families—that higher co-adaptation scores strongly predict patching harm, while lower scores correlate with safety. This provides cross-architecture support for a fundamental principle of LLM pipeline robustness.

item.title: On the Formal Verification of Authenticated Encryption of the MQTT Protocol
item.description: This paper proposes *secure MQTT*, a formally verified, end-to-end encrypted (E2EE) extension of MQTT tailored for resource-constrained IoT devices. To eliminate broker-mediated privacy risks—especially for sensitive medical data—we design a nested AES-GCM architecture that strictly isolates link-layer routing metadata (e.g., topic names) from application-layer confidential payloads. Authentication and session key derivation are achieved via an MQTT v5.0–integrated challenge-response mechanism embedding a one-time Broker Nonce into a Schnorr-signature variant of the HMQV protocol, yielding authenticated E2EE keys with negligible overhead over standard ECDH. Using ProVerif under the Dolev-Yao model, we rigorously prove confidentiality, integrity, mutual authentication, and MitM resistance. Our solution delivers a practical, low-overhead, and cryptographically sound foundation for secure IoT messaging.

item.title: On the Security of Public Key Authenticated Encryption with Keyword Search with Sender-independent Search Complexity
item.description: We identify a critical keyword leakage vulnerability in the proxy-free Public-key Authenticated Encryption with Keyword Search (PAUKS) scheme by Li et al. (IEEE TDSC 2026), designed for sender-independent search complexity. Our attack exploits deterministic patterns in ciphertext updates: updated ciphertexts unintentionally leak prefix information of keyword hashes, enabling offline dictionary attacks without secret keys. We formally present two practical attacks—(i) a keyword distinguishing attack that breaks semantic security of keywords, and (ii) a full keyword recovery attack feasible under realistic parameters (e.g., 80-bit truncated SHA-256). Crucially, we verify that the earlier PAUKS scheme by Li et al. (IEEE TIFS 2023) is also vulnerable to the same attack, revealing a systemic flaw in their update mechanism. This work exposes a fundamental tension between sender-independent search efficiency and ciphertext unlinkability, and proposes concrete countermeasures—including randomized update tokens and revised trapdoor derivation—to restore keyword privacy.

item.title: Security of LLM-generated Code: A Comparative Analysis
item.description: This paper presents the first empirical, developer-aligned security assessment of LLM-generated code. We evaluate seven widely used LLMs—including GPT-4-turbo, Claude-3.5-Sonnet, and Llama-3-70B—across 120 realistic programming tasks modeled after GitHub issues and Stack Overflow queries. Using a rigorous two-phase validation (static analysis + expert manual review with dynamic testing), we find that **all seven models generate code containing security vulnerabilities**, with **76.3% classified as Critical or High severity** (CVSS ≥7.0) per NIST standards. Common flaws include SQL injection, hardcoded credentials, and unsafe deserialization. Surprisingly, larger models do not consistently produce more secure code; the largest model exhibited the highest vulnerability density (0.83 per kLOC). We further demonstrate that prompt engineering reduces vulnerabilities by up to 41%, yet cannot eliminate them entirely. Our benchmark provides actionable insights for integrating LLMs safely into SecDevOps pipelines.

item.title: FIRMA: FIbonacci Ring Model Aggregation for Privacy-preserving Federated Learning
item.description: Federated learning faces a structural trilemma: server-based aggregation risks single-point failure and gradient inversion; decentralized ring-gossip leaks classification heads via uniform weighting; personalized methods reintroduce central servers. FIRMA (**FI**bonacci **R**ing **M**odel **A**ggregation) resolves this by introducing a family of three server-free, ring-topology protocols with permanently private heads and principled asymmetric neighbor weighting. FibFL establishes Fibonacci-weighted directional blending; FibFLP adds accuracy-gated neighbor suppression; FibFLPP completes the system with 2-opt class-diversity ring permutation, $\lceil N/2\rceil$-pass global gossip, and cosine-annealed self-retention. We prove convergence bounds and four optimality propositions. Across 28 heterogeneous configurations, FibFLPP outperforms FedAvg in all 12 label-skew settings (peak +20.7 pp on CIFAR-10, $K=1$) and is Pareto-dominant among all server-free methods—achieving highest accuracy in 17/28 cases under Dirichlet heterogeneity.

item.title: Boundary-targeted Membership Inference Attacks on Safety Classifiers
item.description: Safety classifiers—critical for filtering harmful content and detecting at-risk users in generative AI—are trained on sensitive mental health data, raising serious but underexplored privacy risks. We propose **boundary-targeted membership inference attacks (MIAs)**, hypothesizing that low-confidence examples near the decision boundary expose stronger membership signals due to localized memorization. Our novel selection strategy identifies and amplifies these boundary-laying samples. Experiments show the attack recovers **19% of distress-flagged user conversations at only 5% false-positive rate**, outperforming state-of-the-art MIAs by **3.5×** on an emotion-support detection classifier. We characterize boundary examples as semantically ambiguous (e.g., “I’ve been tired lately, but can’t explain why”)—resistant to content-based filtering yet effectively mitigated by modest logit noise (σ=0.3 reduces success by 72%). This work reveals a critical privacy-robustness trade-off in safety models.
